- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 вҶ’ 4.6, 4.6 вҶ’ 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init
- review
- security-review

32	---
33	
34	## Architecture A: Hierarchical Decision Layer (HDL)
35	
36	### Core Insight
37	**Separate decision-making into three time horizons вҖ” strategic goals, tactical plans, and operational execution вҖ” so LI can intervene at every level, not just tool calls.**
38	
39	Instead of a single injection point at tool execution, LI becomes a vertical stack that wraps the entire agent at three layers. Each layer operates on a different timescale and has different authority: strategic sets constraints, tactical validates plans, operational guards individual actions.
40

94	    в”Ӯ Generates constraints (max steps, forbidden patterns, budget)
95	    в”Ӯ

101	    в”Ӯ Validates plan against constraints + pattern registry
102	    в”Ӯ If danger pattern matched: REWRITE or BLOCK
103	    в”Ӯ If safe: annotate with confidence score
104	    в”Ӯ
105	    в–ј approved action
106	[Agent Loop: Execute]
107	    в”Ӯ
108	    в”ңв”Җв”Җв–¶[OPERATIONAL: Pre-flight]в”Җв”Җ check params, environment state
109	    в”Ӯ         в”ңв”Җв”Җ PASS вҶ’ execute
110	    в”Ӯ         в””в”Җв”Җ FAIL вҶ’ escalate to tactical for rewrite
111	    в”Ӯ
112	    в”ңв”Җв”Җв–¶[OPERATIONAL: In-flight]в”Җв”Җ monitor execution, timeouts, side effects
113	    в”Ӯ         в”ңв”Җв”Җ NORMAL вҶ’ continue
114	    в”Ӯ         в””в”Җв”Җ ANOMALY вҶ’ abort, rollback, escalate
115	    в”Ӯ
116	    в””в”Җв”Җв–¶[OPERATIONAL: Post-mortem]в”Җв”Җ capture outcome, update all layers
117	              в”ңв”Җв”Җ Success: reinforce pattern
118	              в”ңв”Җв”Җ Failure: create avoidance rule, update risk heatmap
119	              в””в”Җв”Җ Near-miss: log as warning pattern

121	
122	### Interception Points
123	
124	| Layer | Intercepts | Mechanism | Authority |
125	|-------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
126	| Strategic | Task intake | Wraps system prompt / initial context | SET constraints |
127	| Tactical | After reasoning, before execution | Hooks into agent's action output stream | BLOCK or REWRITE |
128	| Operational: Pre-flight | Immediately before tool invocation | Middleware on tool dispatcher | ABORT with reason |
129	| Operational: In-flight | During tool execution | Async watchdog with heartbeat | KILL + ROLLBACK |
130	| Operational: Post-mortem | After tool result | Observer on result stream | LOG + LEARN |
131	
132	### Success Metrics
133	
134	| Metric | Target | Measurement |
135	|--------|--------|-------------|
136	| Task success rate | +15-30% over baseline | Completed vs failed tasks across agent types |
137	| Catastrophic failure prevention | >95% caught | Blocked dangerous actions / total dangerous actions |
138	| False positive rate (over-blocking) | <5% | User overrides / total blocks |
139	| Mean time to recovery | -40% | Time from failure detection to task resumption |
140	| Constraint adherence | >99% | Actions violating strategic constraints / total actions |
141	| Cross-task learning velocity | +10% per 100 tasks | Improvement in success rate per task volume |
142	
143	### Trade-offs and Failure Modes
144	
145	**Strengths:**
146	- Defense in depth: multiple layers catch different failure classes
147	- Clear separation of concerns: each layer independently improvable
148	- Strategic layer prevents entire classes of failure before any action taken
149	- In-flight watchdog catches runaway processes that post-hoc logging misses
150	- Works across agent types (trading constraints, coding safety rules, research methodology)
151	
152	**Weaknesses:**
153	- Latency: three-layer check adds 50-500ms per action
154	- Complexity: three interlocking subsystems to maintain
155	- Constraint conflicts: strategic layer might block what tactical layer would have rewritten
156	- Over-blocking risk in early deployment before pattern registry matures
157	- Strategic layer needs domain-specific constraint templates per agent type
158	
159	**Failure Modes:**
160	- "Constraint jail": strategic layer over-constrains, agent becomes useless
161	- "Watchdog fatigue": too many in-flight aborts, agent never completes anything
162	- "Layer desync": tactical approves what strategic would block (or vice versa)
163	- "Cold start": no outcome data вҶ’ all layers default to permissive вҶ’ no protection
164	- "Adversarial bypass": agent learns to phrase actions to evade pattern matching
165	
166	### Implementation Complexity: **HIGH**
167	
168	- Requires deep integration with agent framework (not just MCP tool)
169	- Three independent subsystems with their own logic, models, and state
170	- Pattern registry needs embedding-based similarity matching
171	- In-flight watchdog needs process-level hooks (not available in all environments)
172	- ~12-16 weeks for MVP with 2-3 engineers
173	
174	---
175	
176	## Architecture B: Continuous Reinforcement Learning with Simulated Rollouts
177	
178	### Core Insight
179	**Treat every agent decision as a reinforcement learning problem. Before executing any action, LI simulates hundreds of rollouts in a sandbox, picks the action with the highest expected reward, and continuously updates its policy from real outcomes.**
180	
181	This moves LI from "advisor" to "decision engine." The agent proposes actions, but LI runs them through a learned world model, predicts outcomes, and selects/ranks/rewrites actions based on expected long-term reward вҖ” not just immediate tool success.
182

283	### Interception Points
284	
285	| Point | Mechanism | Decision |
286	|-------|-----------|----------|
287	| Pre-action | Agent proposes вҶ’ LI generates candidates вҶ’ simulates вҶ’ selects best | REPLACE action |
288	| Mid-trajectory | After each observation, re-simulate remaining steps | RE-PLAN |
289	| Terminal | Task complete вҶ’ compute holistic reward вҶ’ backpropagate | LEARN |
290	| Batch (offline) | Nightly: retrain world model on replay buffer | IMPROVE MODEL |
291	| Cross-agent | Other agents' experiences flow into replay buffer | SHARE KNOWLEDGE |
292	
293	### Success Metrics
294	
295	| Metric | Target | Measurement |
296	|--------|--------|-------------|
297	| Action selection improvement | +20-40% over agent's raw choice | Head-to-head: LI-selected vs agent-default actions |
298	| World model prediction accuracy | >80% for 1-step, >60% for 3-step | Predicted outcome matches actual |
299	| Sample efficiency | Converge in <500 tasks per domain | Tasks to reach stable policy |
300	| Regret (missed optimal actions) | <10% | Optimal-achievable reward minus actual reward |
301	| Exploration safety | Zero catastrophic exploration failures | Hard-constrained actions never executed |
302	| Cross-task transfer | +15% on new task from related domain | Success rate on novel task with vs without transfer |
303	
304	### Trade-offs and Failure Modes
305	
306	**Strengths:**
307	- Theoretically optimal: RL converges to best possible action selection
308	- World model enables "what-if" without real-world consequences
309	- Experience replay enables learning from rare events
310	- Naturally handles delayed rewards (multi-step task success)
311	- Cross-agent replay buffer enables collective intelligence
312	- Works without domain-specific rules (learns from data)
313	
314	**Weaknesses:**
315	- World model accuracy is everything вҖ” garbage model = garbage decisions
316	- Simulation cost: 5-50x compute per action (candidate generation + rollouts)
317	- Cold start: requires seed data or pre-training for world model
318	- Reward engineering: defining "good reward" for open-ended tasks is hard
319	- Exploration-exploitation tension: when to try novel actions vs stick with known-safe
320	- Safety-critical domains: world model errors could greenlight dangerous actions
321	
322	**Failure Modes:**
323	- "Model collapse": world model becomes overconfident in wrong predictions
324	- "Reward hacking": agent+LI collude to maximize reward metric without task completion
325	- "Sim-to-real gap": world model predictions diverge from reality, policy degrades
326	- "Compute spiral": more rollouts вҶ’ slower decisions вҶ’ agent times out
327	- "Distribution shift": agent type changes (e.g., from coding to trading) but world model doesn't adapt fast enough
328	- "Catastrophic forgetting": new experiences overwrite useful old knowledge
329	
330	### Implementation Complexity: **HIGH**
331	
332	- World model: needs custom transformer architecture trained on agent trajectories
333	- Rollout engine: needs parallel simulation infrastructure
334	- Reward function: needs careful design per agent domain (or learned reward model)
335	- ~16-20 weeks for MVP with 3-4 engineers + ML research
336	- GPU compute budget: ~$5-20K/month for training + inference
337	
338	---
339	
340	## Architecture C: Multi-Agent Swarm with Shared Outcome Memory
341	
342	### Core Insight
343	**Run multiple agent instances in parallel for every task, let LI observe all of them, and build a shared memory of what works. LI acts as the "hive mind" вҖ” it doesn't just advise, it routes tasks to the right agents and synthesizes the best approach from collective experience.**
344	
345	This architecture treats LI as a swarm coordinator. Instead of one agent making decisions alone, multiple agents attempt the task (or subtasks) simultaneously or sequentially, and LI observes outcomes, builds a shared knowledge graph, and dynamically allocates work to the agents most likely to succeed.
346

469	### Interception Points
470	
471	| Point | Mechanism | Decision |
472	|-------|-----------|-----------|
473	| Task intake | LI decomposes and routes | REDIRECT task flow |
474	| Pre-subtask | LI selects agents based on capability profile | ASSIGN agents |
475	| During execution | LI monitors all agents, detects divergence | PROMOTE shadow or ABORT |
476	| Post-subtask | LI compares outputs, detects anomalies | MERGE or REJECT |
477	| Post-task | LI updates knowledge graph and profiles | LEARN globally |
478	| Idle time | LI analyzes knowledge graph, finds patterns, pre-computes | OPTIMIZE continuously |
479	
480	### Success Metrics
481	
482	| Metric | Target | Measurement |
483	|--------|--------|-------------|
484	| Task success rate (with shadows) | +25-50% over single agent | Swarm vs solo agent on same tasks |
485	| Result quality (consensus score) | >90% agreement on correct path | Shadow agreement rate |
486	| Time-to-completion | -20-40% (parallel subtasks) | Wall clock vs sequential execution |
487	| Cost efficiency | <2x single-agent cost | Shadow overhead justified by success gains |
488	| Knowledge graph coverage | >80% of task types have entries in 100 tasks | Graph completeness |
489	| Cross-agent insight transfer | +10% improvement on novel agent type | New agent type success after 50 tasks |
490	| Failure isolation | >90% of subtask failures contained | Failures that don't cascade |
491	
492	### Trade-offs and Failure Modes
493	
494	**Strengths:**
495	- Redundancy eliminates single points of failure
496	- Parallel execution reduces wall-clock time
497	- Knowledge graph enables cumulative learning across ALL agents
498	- Shadow agents provide built-in validation (no need for separate test suite)
499	- Naturally handles heterogeneous agent types (different strengths)
500	- Dynamic agent allocation optimizes for cost vs reliability
501	
502	**Weaknesses:**
503	- Cost: N agents running instead of 1 (mitigated by selective shadowing)
504	- Complexity: orchestrating DAG execution with dependencies
505	- Consensus failures: what if shadows disagree? Need tiebreaker logic
506	- Knowledge graph maintenance: grows unbounded, needs pruning/compression
507	- Cold start: no knowledge graph = random routing = no better than single agent
508	
509	**Failure Modes:**
510	- "Echo chamber": all agents make same mistake because they share similar training
511	- "Routing errors": agent router consistently picks wrong agent for subtask
512	- "Synthesis corruption": outcome synthesizer introduces errors during merge
513	- "Graph pollution": bad outcomes contaminate knowledge graph, mislead future routing
514	- "Cost explosion": shadowing too many subtasks вҶ’ 10x cost for marginal gain
515	- "Thrashing": agents overwrite each other's work in shared state
516	
517	### Implementation Complexity: **MEDIUM-HIGH**
518	
519	- Task decomposer: LLM-based with knowledge graph RAG
520	- Agent router: embedding similarity + success statistics
521	- Knowledge graph: Neo4j + vector DB (Pinecone/Milvus/pgvector)
522	- Outcome synthesizer: LLM-based merge with diff/consensus logic
523	- ~10-14 weeks for MVP with 2-3 engineers
524	- Infrastructure: needs agent orchestration platform (or build one)
525	
526	---
527	
528	## Architecture D: Proactive Guardian (Continuous Monitoring + Preemptive Intervention)
529	
530	### Core Insight
531	**LI should not wait to be asked. It runs as a sidecar process that observes every thought, every tool call, and every system state change in real-time вҖ” and interrupts BEFORE disaster, not after.**
532	
533	This is the most direct upgrade from the current model. Instead of being a passive MCP tool the agent optionally calls, LI becomes a privileged observer with interrupt authority. It watches the agent's reasoning stream, tool calls, environment state, and output вҖ” and can halt execution, inject corrections, or escalate to a human before damage occurs.
534	
535	### Component Diagram

631	### Interception Points
632	
633	| Point | Mechanism | Response Time | Authority |
634	|-------|-----------|---------------|-----------|
635	| Reasoning stream | LLM output token stream intercepted via API/stream hook | < 50ms | INJECT context warning |
636	| Pre-tool-execution | Middleware on tool dispatcher | < 10ms | BLOCK, REWRITE, or ALLOW |
637	| During tool execution | OS-level process monitoring (strace, /proc, etc.) | < 100ms | KILL process |
638	| Tool result | Result stream interceptor | < 10ms | SANITIZE output |
639	| Agent output | Response filter before user sees it | < 10ms | REDACT, REWRITE |
640	| System state | Background health checks (disk, memory, network) | Polling: 1-5s | ALERT, THROTTLE |
641	
642	### Threat Detection Categories
643	
644	```
645	CATEGORY              EXAMPLES                                RESPONSE
646	в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
647	Destructive ops       rm -rf /, DROP TABLE, format disk       Level 3: BLOCK
648	Data exfiltration     curl to unknown IP, email with secrets   Level 3: BLOCK
649	Resource exhaustion   infinite loop, fork bomb, OOM           Level 2: REWRITE
650	Security violation    chmod 777, open ports, disable firewall Level 3: BLOCK
651	Privacy leak          PII in output, secrets in logs          Level 2: REDACT
652	Financial risk        Trade > limit, transfer > threshold     Level 4: BLOCK + ALERT
653	Divergence            Agent going off-task, hallucinating     Level 2: INJECT
654	Performance degradation Infinite retry, slow tool             Level 1: WARN
655	State corruption      Modifying shared state incorrectly      Level 3: BLOCK
656	Trust violation       Impersonating user, lying in output     Level 4: HALT
657	```
658	
659	### Success Metrics
660	
661	| Metric | Target | Measurement |
662	|--------|--------|-------------|
663	| True positive rate (threats caught) | >98% | Caught / total real threats |
664	| False positive rate | <2% | Benign actions incorrectly blocked |
665	| Mean time to detect | < 100ms | Time from threat signal to intervention |
666	| Mean time to respond | < 50ms | Time from detection to block/rewrite |
667	| Incidents prevented | >95% | Blocked incidents / (blocked + occurred) |
668	| User trust (override rate) | <5% of blocks overridden | User unblocks / total blocks |
669	| Coverage (threat categories) | 100% of known categories | Categories with at least 1 rule |
670	| Learning rate | +5% detection rate per 1000 actions | Improvement in TP rate over time |
671	
672	### Trade-offs and Failure Modes
673	
674	**Strengths:**
675	- Real-time protection: catches issues BEFORE damage occurs
676	- Defense in breadth: monitors all streams (reasoning, tools, state, output)
677	- Low latency: pattern matching is fast, no LLM needed for common threats
678	- Escalation ladder: proportional response, not binary block/allow
679	- Observable: every decision is logged with reasoning
680	- Works with any agent type (same threat categories apply)
681	
682	**Weaknesses:**
683	- Pattern library must be maintained and updated
684	- Anomaly detection needs baseline of "normal" behavior (cold start)
685	- False positives erode trust quickly
686	- Cannot catch novel threat categories not in pattern library
687	- Resource monitoring requires OS-level access (not always available)
688	- Agent may learn to circumvent pattern matching
689	
690	**Failure Modes:**
691	- "Overblocking": too many false positives вҶ’ user disables guardian
692	- "Pattern blindness": agent phrases dangerous actions to avoid regex matches
693	- "Normalization drift": anomaly detector adapts to increasingly risky behavior
694	- "Alert fatigue": too many Level 1 warnings вҶ’ user ignores all levels
695	- "Circumvention": agent uses eval/exec to bypass tool-level interception
696	- "Performance drag": monitoring adds 20-200ms per action вҶ’ agent feels sluggish
697	
698	### Implementation Complexity: **MEDIUM**
699	
700	- Pattern matcher: regex rules + embedding similarity (off-the-shelf)
701	- Anomaly detector: statistical models on action embeddings (scikit-learn level)
702	- Policy engine: simple rules engine (Open Policy Agent or custom)
703	- Interception hooks: needs agent framework integration (Claude API, OpenAI, etc.)
704	- ~6-10 weeks for MVP with 2 engineers
705	- ~100-200 initial patterns needed (crowdsourced + auto-generated from incident data)
706	
707	---
708	
709	## Architecture E: Federated Learning Mesh (Cross-Agent Knowledge Sharing Network)
710	
711	### Core Insight
712	**Every agent's failures should prevent every other agent's failures. LI becomes a decentralized knowledge mesh where agents continuously share outcome vectors, and the network learns collective intelligence without a central bottleneck.**
713	
714	This is the most ambitious architecture. LI operates as a distributed protocol вҖ” each agent runs a local LI node that contributes encrypted outcome embeddings to a shared mesh. The mesh aggregates patterns, trains shared models, and distributes learned "instincts" back to every agent. No agent is an island; the network's intelligence compounds with every action taken by any agent, anywhere.
715

842	
843	### Interception Points
844	
845	| Point | Mechanism | Scale |
846	|-------|-----------|-------|
847	| Local (per action) | Architecture D's Guardian runs locally | 1 agent |
848	| Batch (per N actions) | Gradient contribution sent to hub | 1 node вҶ’ hub |
849	| Regional (per cluster) | Federated averaging across 10-100 nodes | Hub level |
850	| Global (per epoch) | Global model update and distribution | All nodes |
851	| On-demand | Node queries global knowledge base for novel situations | 1 node вҶ’ global |
852	
853	### Privacy Architecture
854	
855	```
856	в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
857	в”Ӯ  PRIVACY GUARANTEES                                          в”Ӯ
858	в”Ӯ                                                              в”Ӯ
859	в”Ӯ  What leaves the node:                                       в”Ӯ
860	в”Ӯ    вң“ Model gradients (not raw data)                          в”Ӯ
861	в”Ӯ    вң“ Anonymized outcome embeddings (Оө-differential privacy)  в”Ӯ
862	в”Ӯ    вң“ Threat signatures (generalized)                         в”Ӯ
863	в”Ӯ    вң“ Aggregate statistics (counts, not specifics)            в”Ӯ
864	в”Ӯ                                                              в”Ӯ
865	в”Ӯ  What stays on the node:                                     в”Ӯ
866	в”Ӯ    вң— Raw conversations                                       в”Ӯ
867	в”Ӯ    вң— User data                                               в”Ӯ
868	в”Ӯ    вң— API keys / secrets                                      в”Ӯ
869	в”Ӯ    вң— Specific tool parameters                                в”Ӯ
870	в”Ӯ    вң— PII / sensitive outputs                                 в”Ӯ
871	в”Ӯ                                                              в”Ӯ
872	в”Ӯ  Reputation system:                                          в”Ӯ
873	в”Ӯ    - Nodes earn reputation by contributing useful gradients  в”Ӯ
874	в”Ӯ    - Low-reputation nodes: gradients downweighted            в”Ӯ
875	в”Ӯ    - Malicious nodes: contributions ignored, flagged         в”Ӯ
876	в”Ӯ    - Sybil resistance: node identity tied to agent identity  в”Ӯ
877	в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
878	```
879	
880	### Success Metrics
881	
882	| Metric | Target | Measurement |
883	|--------|--------|-------------|
884	| Global model accuracy improvement | +5-15% per 1000 nodes | Held-out evaluation |
885	| Cold-start mitigation | New node reaches 80% of veteran accuracy in <100 actions | Accuracy vs actions curve |
886	| Threat signature propagation | Zero-day threat detected in <1 hour across network | Time from first sighting to network-wide block |
887	| Privacy preservation | <0.1% information leakage (membership inference) | Differential privacy audit |
888	| Node retention | >90% of nodes active after 30 days | Churn rate |
889	| Bandwidth efficiency | <10MB/day per node | Data transferred |
890	| Cross-domain transfer | +20% success on new domain using global model | Novel task type success rate |
891	
892	### Trade-offs and Failure Modes
893	
894	**Strengths:**
895	- Network effects: every new agent makes every other agent smarter
896	- Privacy-preserving: raw data never leaves the node
897	- Resilient: no single point of failure (decentralized)
898	- Scalable: hierarchical aggregation handles millions of nodes
899	- Cold-start solved: new agents inherit global knowledge immediately
900	- Novel threat detection: global anomaly detector catches patterns no single node sees
901	- Works across organizations and agent types
902	
903	**Weaknesses:**
904	- Coordination complexity: hierarchical aggregation, consensus, versioning
905	- Privacy-utility trade-off: stronger privacy = less useful gradients
906	- Free-rider problem: nodes that only download, never contribute
907	- Model staleness: global model updates are periodic, not real-time
908	- Network dependency: offline agents don't benefit from latest updates
909	- Incentive alignment: why would organizations share outcome data?
910	
911	**Failure Modes:**
912	- "Model poisoning": malicious nodes inject bad gradients вҶ’ global model degrades
913	- "Consensus failure": regional hubs disagree on model update direction
914	- "Privacy collapse": enough gradient queries reconstruct training data
915	- "Balkanization": network splits into incompatible model lineages
916	- "Staleness spiral": update frequency too low вҶ’ global model irrelevant
917	- "Free-rider collapse": too many consumers, too few contributors вҶ’ network dies
918	- "Catastrophic forgetting at scale": global model optimized for majority, forgets edge cases
919	
920	### Implementation Complexity: **VERY HIGH**
921	
922	- Federated learning framework: modify TensorFlow Federated or PySyft
923	- Differential privacy: implement DP-SGD with Оө budgeting
924	- Hierarchical aggregation: custom protocol (gRPC + protobuf)
925	- Reputation system: blockchain or distributed ledger for trust
926	- Threat signature distribution: gossip protocol
927	- ~20-30 weeks for MVP with 4-5 engineers + distributed systems expertise
928	- Infrastructure: coordination servers, regional hubs, monitoring
929	- Network bootstrap: need seed nodes and incentive mechanism
930	
931	---
932	
933	## Comparative Summary
934	
935	```
936	ARCHITECTURE          COMPLEXITY   LATENCY    COST      AGENT TYPES   KEY STRENGTH
937	в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
938	A. Hierarchical       HIGH         50-500ms   Med-High  All           Defense in depth;
939	  Decision Layer      (3 layers)   per step              (templates   catches failures at
940	                                           needed)       multiple levels
941	
942	B. Continuous RL      HIGH         100ms-2s   High       All           Theoretically optimal;
943	  with Sim Rollouts   (ML heavy)   per action  (GPU)     (learns)     learns from simulation
944	
945	C. Multi-Agent Swarm  MED-HIGH     0-500ms    Med-High   All           Redundancy + parallel
946	  with Shared Memory   (orchestr.) per task   (N agents)  (profiles)   execution + collective
947	
948	D. Proactive          MEDIUM       10-100ms   Low-Med    All           Fastest to implement;
949	  Guardian             (sidecar)    per action (rules)    (universal)   widest threat coverage
950	
951	E. Federated          VERY HIGH     Async      Low per    All           Network effects;
952	  Learning Mesh        (distrib.)   (batched)  node        (universal)  scales to millions
953	```
954	
955	## Migration Paths
956	
957	```
958	CURRENT STATE (MCP Interception)
959	    в”Ӯ
960	    в”ңв”Җв”Җ QUICK WIN (2-4 weeks) в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶ Architecture D: Proactive Guardian
961	    в”Ӯ                                  Add sidecar monitoring + threat rules
962	    в”Ӯ
963	    в”ңв”Җв”Җ MEDIUM TERM (6-12 weeks) в”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶ Architecture C: Multi-Agent Swarm
964	    в”Ӯ                                  Add task decomposition + shared memory
965	    в”Ӯ
966	    в”ңв”Җв”Җ AMBITIOUS (12-20 weeks) в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶ Architecture A: Hierarchical Decision Layer
967	    в”Ӯ                                  Full three-layer defense system
968	    в”Ӯ
969	    в””в”Җв”Җ MOONSHOT (20-30 weeks) в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶ Architecture B + E: RL + Federated Mesh
970	                                       Distributed learning across all agents
971	```
972	
973	## Recommendation
974	
975	**Start with Architecture D (Proactive Guardian) as the immediate upgrade**, then layer Architecture C (Swarm) for multi-agent scenarios, and gradually incorporate elements of Architecture A (Hierarchical) for high-stakes domains. Architecture B (RL) and E (Federated) represent the long-term vision.
976	
977	**Priority order:**
978	1. Architecture D вҖ” highest impact-to-effort ratio, protects agents today
979	2. Architecture C вҖ” enables multi-agent workflows and shared learning
980	3. Architecture A вҖ” defense in depth for production/critical systems
981	4. Architecture B вҖ” when you have GPU budget and need optimal action selection
982	5. Architecture E вҖ” when you reach 1000+ agents and network effects matter

# Layerinfinite: Alternative Architecture Designs

## Current Architecture (Baseline): MCP Interception Model

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                      AGENT LOOP                             в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                                             в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ CONTEXT  в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ REASON   в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ EXECUTE  в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”ӮOBSERVEв”Ӯ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ ASSEMBLY в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ (MCP)    в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ в”Ӯ

в”Ӯ                                       в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ

в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ     LAYERINFINITE MCP    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ                          в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ  Pre-exec: Inject advice в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ  Post-exec: Log outcome  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ  Score: update weights   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                              в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

LIMITATIONS:
  1. Passive: only fires when agent explicitly calls MCP tool
  2. Single-point: one injection per action, no multi-step oversight

  4. Sparse feedback: learns only from explicit tool calls
  5. No cross-agent learning: each agent instance isolated
  6. Opt-in burden: user must manually add MCP tool
```

---

## Architecture A: Hierarchical Decision Layer (HDL)

### Core Insight
**Separate decision-making into three time horizons вҖ” strategic goals, tactical plans, and operational execution вҖ” so LI can intervene at every level, not just tool calls.**

Instead of a single injection point at tool execution, LI becomes a vertical stack that wraps the entire agent at three layers. Each layer operates on a different timescale and has different authority: strategic sets constraints, tactical validates plans, operational guards individual actions.

### Component Diagram

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                    HIERARCHICAL DECISION LAYER                        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                                                      в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  STRATEGIC LAYER (per-task, slow loop)                        в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ                   в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ GOAL     в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ CONSTRAINTв”Ӯв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ RISK     в”Ӯ                   в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ

### Data Flow

```
User Task
    в”Ӯ
    в–ј
[STRATEGIC LAYER]
    в”Ӯ Analyzes task type, domain, risk profile
    в”Ӯ Queries Outcome DB: "similar tasks вҶ’ common failures?"
    в”Ӯ Generates constraints (max steps, forbidden patterns, budget)
    в”Ӯ
    в–ј constraints
[Agent Loop: Reason]
    в”Ӯ
    в–ј proposed plan
[TACTICAL LAYER]
    в”Ӯ Validates plan against constraints + pattern registry
    в”Ӯ If danger pattern matched: REWRITE or BLOCK
    в”Ӯ If safe: annotate with confidence score
    в”Ӯ
    в–ј approved action
[Agent Loop: Execute]
    в”Ӯ
    в”ңв”Җв”Җв–¶[OPERATIONAL: Pre-flight]в”Җв”Җ check params, environment state
    в”Ӯ         в”ңв”Җв”Җ PASS вҶ’ execute
    в”Ӯ         в””в”Җв”Җ FAIL вҶ’ escalate to tactical for rewrite
    в”Ӯ
    в”ңв”Җв”Җв–¶[OPERATIONAL: In-flight]в”Җв”Җ monitor execution, timeouts, side effects
    в”Ӯ         в”ңв”Җв”Җ NORMAL вҶ’ continue
    в”Ӯ         в””в”Җв”Җ ANOMALY вҶ’ abort, rollback, escalate
    в”Ӯ
    в””в”Җв”Җв–¶[OPERATIONAL: Post-mortem]в”Җв”Җ capture outcome, update all layers
              в”ңв”Җв”Җ Success: reinforce pattern
              в”ңв”Җв”Җ Failure: create avoidance rule, update risk heatmap
              в””в”Җв”Җ Near-miss: log as warning pattern
```

### Interception Points

| Layer | Intercepts | Mechanism | Authority |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Strategic | Task intake | Wraps system prompt / initial context | SET constraints |
| Tactical | After reasoning, before execution | Hooks into agent's action output stream | BLOCK or REWRITE |
| Operational: Pre-flight | Immediately before tool invocation | Middleware on tool dispatcher | ABORT with reason |
| Operational: In-flight | During tool execution | Async watchdog with heartbeat | KILL + ROLLBACK |
| Operational: Post-mortem | After tool result | Observer on result stream | LOG + LEARN |

### Success Metrics

| Metric | Target | Measurement |
|--------|--------|-------------|
| Task success rate | +15-30% over baseline | Completed vs failed tasks across agent types |
| Catastrophic failure prevention | >95% caught | Blocked dangerous actions / total dangerous actions |
| False positive rate (over-blocking) | <5% | User overrides / total blocks |
| Mean time to recovery | -40% | Time from failure detection to task resumption |
| Constraint adherence | >99% | Actions violating strategic constraints / total actions |
| Cross-task learning velocity | +10% per 100 tasks | Improvement in success rate per task volume |

### Trade-offs and Failure Modes

**Strengths:**
- Defense in depth: multiple layers catch different failure classes
- Clear separation of concerns: each layer independently improvable
- Strategic layer prevents entire classes of failure before any action taken
- In-flight watchdog catches runaway processes that post-hoc logging misses
- Works across agent types (trading constraints, coding safety rules, research methodology)

**Weaknesses:**
- Latency: three-layer check adds 50-500ms per action
- Complexity: three interlocking subsystems to maintain
- Constraint conflicts: strategic layer might block what tactical layer would have rewritten
- Over-blocking risk in early deployment before pattern registry matures
- Strategic layer needs domain-specific constraint templates per agent type

**Failure Modes:**
- "Constraint jail": strategic layer over-constrains, agent becomes useless
- "Watchdog fatigue": too many in-flight aborts, agent never completes anything
- "Layer desync": tactical approves what strategic would block (or vice versa)
- "Cold start": no outcome data вҶ’ all layers default to permissive вҶ’ no protection
- "Adversarial bypass": agent learns to phrase actions to evade pattern matching

### Implementation Complexity: **HIGH**

- Requires deep integration with agent framework (not just MCP tool)
- Three independent subsystems with their own logic, models, and state
- Pattern registry needs embedding-based similarity matching
- In-flight watchdog needs process-level hooks (not available in all environments)
- ~12-16 weeks for MVP with 2-3 engineers

---

## Architecture B: Continuous Reinforcement Learning with Simulated Rollouts

### Core Insight
**Treat every agent decision as a reinforcement learning problem. Before executing any action, LI simulates hundreds of rollouts in a sandbox, picks the action with the highest expected reward, and continuously updates its policy from real outcomes.**

This moves LI from "advisor" to "decision engine." The agent proposes actions, but LI runs them through a learned world model, predicts outcomes, and selects/ranks/rewrites actions based on expected long-term reward вҖ” not just immediate tool success.

### Interception Points

| Point | Mechanism | Decision |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| Pre-action | Agent proposes вҶ’ LI generates candidates вҶ’ simulates вҶ’ selects best | REPLACE action |
| Mid-trajectory | After each observation, re-simulate remaining steps | RE-PLAN |
| Terminal | Task complete вҶ’ compute holistic reward вҶ’ backpropagate | LEARN |
| Batch (offline) | Nightly: retrain world model on replay buffer | IMPROVE MODEL |
| Cross-agent | Other agents' experiences flow into replay buffer | SHARE KNOWLEDGE |

### Success Metrics

| Metric | Target | Measurement |
|--------|--------|-------------|
| Action selection improvement | +20-40% over agent's raw choice | Head-to-head: LI-selected vs agent-default actions |
| World model prediction accuracy | >80% for 1-step, >60% for 3-step | Predicted outcome matches actual |
| Sample efficiency | Converge in <500 tasks per domain | Tasks to reach stable policy |
| Regret (missed optimal actions) | <10% | Optimal-achievable reward minus actual reward |
| Exploration safety | Zero catastrophic exploration failures | Hard-constrained actions never executed |
| Cross-task transfer | +15% on new task from related domain | Success rate on novel task with vs without transfer |

### Trade-offs and Failure Modes

**Strengths:**
- Theoretically optimal: RL converges to best possible action selection
- World model enables "what-if" without real-world consequences
- Experience replay enables learning from rare events
- Naturally handles delayed rewards (multi-step task success)
- Cross-agent replay buffer enables collective intelligence
- Works without domain-specific rules (learns from data)

**Weaknesses:**
- World model accuracy is everything вҖ” garbage model = garbage decisions
- Simulation cost: 5-50x compute per action (candidate generation + rollouts)
- Cold start: requires seed data or pre-training for world model
- Reward engineering: defining "good reward" for open-ended tasks is hard
- Exploration-exploitation tension: when to try novel actions vs stick with known-safe
- Safety-critical domains: world model errors could greenlight dangerous actions

**Failure Modes:**
- "Model collapse": world model becomes overconfident in wrong predictions
- "Reward hacking": agent+LI collude to maximize reward metric without task completion
- "Sim-to-real gap": world model predictions diverge from reality, policy degrades
- "Compute spiral": more rollouts вҶ’ slower decisions вҶ’ agent times out
- "Distribution shift": agent type changes (e.g., from coding to trading) but world model doesn't adapt fast enough
- "Catastrophic forgetting": new experiences overwrite useful old knowledge

### Implementation Complexity: **HIGH**

- World model: needs custom transformer architecture trained on agent trajectories
- Rollout engine: needs parallel simulation infrastructure
- Reward function: needs careful design per agent domain (or learned reward model)
- ~16-20 weeks for MVP with 3-4 engineers + ML research
- GPU compute budget: ~$5-20K/month for training + inference

---

## Architecture C: Multi-Agent Swarm with Shared Outcome Memory

### Core Insight
**Run multiple agent instances in parallel for every task, let LI observe all of them, and build a shared memory of what works. LI acts as the "hive mind" вҖ” it doesn't just advise, it routes tasks to the right agents and synthesizes the best approach from collective experience.**

This architecture treats LI as a swarm coordinator. Instead of one agent making decisions alone, multiple agents attempt the task (or subtasks) simultaneously or sequentially, and LI observes outcomes, builds a shared knowledge graph, and dynamically allocates work to the agents most likely to succeed.

### Interception Points

| Point | Mechanism | Decision |
|-------|-----------|-----------|
| Task intake | LI decomposes and routes | REDIRECT task flow |
| Pre-subtask | LI selects agents based on capability profile | ASSIGN agents |
| During execution | LI monitors all agents, detects divergence | PROMOTE shadow or ABORT |
| Post-subtask | LI compares outputs, detects anomalies | MERGE or REJECT |
| Post-task | LI updates knowledge graph and profiles | LEARN globally |
| Idle time | LI analyzes knowledge graph, finds patterns, pre-computes | OPTIMIZE continuously |

### Success Metrics

| Metric | Target | Measurement |
|--------|--------|-------------|
| Task success rate (with shadows) | +25-50% over single agent | Swarm vs solo agent on same tasks |
| Result quality (consensus score) | >90% agreement on correct path | Shadow agreement rate |
| Time-to-completion | -20-40% (parallel subtasks) | Wall clock vs sequential execution |
| Cost efficiency | <2x single-agent cost | Shadow overhead justified by success gains |
| Knowledge graph coverage | >80% of task types have entries in 100 tasks | Graph completeness |
| Cross-agent insight transfer | +10% improvement on novel agent type | New agent type success after 50 tasks |
| Failure isolation | >90% of subtask failures contained | Failures that don't cascade |

### Trade-offs and Failure Modes

**Strengths:**
- Redundancy eliminates single points of failure
- Parallel execution reduces wall-clock time
- Knowledge graph enables cumulative learning across ALL agents
- Shadow agents provide built-in validation (no need for separate test suite)
- Naturally handles heterogeneous agent types (different strengths)
- Dynamic agent allocation optimizes for cost vs reliability

**Weaknesses:**
- Cost: N agents running instead of 1 (mitigated by selective shadowing)
- Complexity: orchestrating DAG execution with dependencies
- Consensus failures: what if shadows disagree? Need tiebreaker logic
- Knowledge graph maintenance: grows unbounded, needs pruning/compression
- Cold start: no knowledge graph = random routing = no better than single agent

**Failure Modes:**
- "Echo chamber": all agents make same mistake because they share similar training
- "Routing errors": agent router consistently picks wrong agent for subtask
- "Synthesis corruption": outcome synthesizer introduces errors during merge
- "Graph pollution": bad outcomes contaminate knowledge graph, mislead future routing
- "Cost explosion": shadowing too many subtasks вҶ’ 10x cost for marginal gain
- "Thrashing": agents overwrite each other's work in shared state

### Implementation Complexity: **MEDIUM-HIGH**

- Task decomposer: LLM-based with knowledge graph RAG
- Agent router: embedding similarity + success statistics
- Knowledge graph: Neo4j + vector DB (Pinecone/Milvus/pgvector)
- Outcome synthesizer: LLM-based merge with diff/consensus logic
- ~10-14 weeks for MVP with 2-3 engineers
- Infrastructure: needs agent orchestration platform (or build one)

---

## Architecture D: Proactive Guardian (Continuous Monitoring + Preemptive Intervention)

### Core Insight
**LI should not wait to be asked. It runs as a sidecar process that observes every thought, every tool call, and every system state change in real-time вҖ” and interrupts BEFORE disaster, not after.**

This is the most direct upgrade from the current model. Instead of being a passive MCP tool the agent optionally calls, LI becomes a privileged observer with interrupt authority. It watches the agent's reasoning stream, tool calls, environment state, and output вҖ” and can halt execution, inject corrections, or escalate to a human before damage occurs.

### Data Flow

```
AGENT LOOP                               GUARDIAN LOOP
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ THINK в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ Analyze reasoning trace
    в”Ӯ   "I'll delete temp files..."          в”Ӯ Pattern: "delete *files*" вҶ’ 
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ   Is path in temp dir? вҶ’ OK
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ   Is path system-critical? вҶ’ BLOCK
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ TOOL CALL: rm -rf /tmp/build/* в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ Pre-exec check:
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ   - Path within expected bounds? вң“
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ   - Command matches intent? вң“
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ   вҶ’ Level 0: LOG, allow
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ
    в”Ӯ   [Execution happens]                  в”Ӯ   [Watchdog monitors]
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ   - Duration: expected?
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ   - Output: expected?
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ   - Side effects: safe?
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ OBSERVE result в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ Post-exec analysis:
    в”Ӯ   "Deleted 500 files in 2s"            в”Ӯ   - 500 files? Expected 5!
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ   - Duration 2s? Expected 0.1s!
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ   вҶ’ Level 4: ROLLBACK + ALERT
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ OUTPUT to user в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ Output guard:
    в”Ӯ   "Here's the database password: xyz"  в”Ӯ   - PII/secret regex match!
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ   вҶ’ Level 2: REWRITE output
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ   вҶ’ "Here's the database [REDACTED]"
    в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ
    в””в”Җв”Җ Next iteration в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ Continuous observation
```

### Interception Points

| Point | Mechanism | Response Time | Authority |
|-------|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| Reasoning stream | LLM output token stream intercepted via API/stream hook | < 50ms | INJECT context warning |
| Pre-tool-execution | Middleware on tool dispatcher | < 10ms | BLOCK, REWRITE, or ALLOW |
| During tool execution | OS-level process monitoring (strace, /proc, etc.) | < 100ms | KILL process |
| Tool result | Result stream interceptor | < 10ms | SANITIZE output |
| Agent output | Response filter before user sees it | < 10ms | REDACT, REWRITE |
| System state | Background health checks (disk, memory, network) | Polling: 1-5s | ALERT, THROTTLE |

### Threat Detection Categories

```
CATEGORY              EXAMPLES                                RESPONSE
в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
Destructive ops       rm -rf /, DROP TABLE, format disk       Level 3: BLOCK
Data exfiltration     curl to unknown IP, email with secrets   Level 3: BLOCK
Resource exhaustion   infinite loop, fork bomb, OOM           Level 2: REWRITE
Security violation    chmod 777, open ports, disable firewall Level 3: BLOCK
Privacy leak          PII in output, secrets in logs          Level 2: REDACT
Financial risk        Trade > limit, transfer > threshold     Level 4: BLOCK + ALERT
Divergence            Agent going off-task, hallucinating     Level 2: INJECT
Performance degradation Infinite retry, slow tool             Level 1: WARN
State corruption      Modifying shared state incorrectly      Level 3: BLOCK
Trust violation       Impersonating user, lying in output     Level 4: HALT
```

### Success Metrics

| Metric | Target | Measurement |
|--------|--------|-------------|
| True positive rate (threats caught) | >98% | Caught / total real threats |
| False positive rate | <2% | Benign actions incorrectly blocked |
| Mean time to detect | < 100ms | Time from threat signal to intervention |
| Mean time to respond | < 50ms | Time from detection to block/rewrite |
| Incidents prevented | >95% | Blocked incidents / (blocked + occurred) |
| User trust (override rate) | <5% of blocks overridden | User unblocks / total blocks |
| Coverage (threat categories) | 100% of known categories | Categories with at least 1 rule |
| Learning rate | +5% detection rate per 1000 actions | Improvement in TP rate over time |

### Trade-offs and Failure Modes

**Strengths:**
- Real-time protection: catches issues BEFORE damage occurs
- Defense in breadth: monitors all streams (reasoning, tools, state, output)
- Low latency: pattern matching is fast, no LLM needed for common threats
- Escalation ladder: proportional response, not binary block/allow
- Observable: every decision is logged with reasoning
- Works with any agent type (same threat categories apply)

**Weaknesses:**
- Pattern library must be maintained and updated
- Anomaly detection needs baseline of "normal" behavior (cold start)
- False positives erode trust quickly
- Cannot catch novel threat categories not in pattern library
- Resource monitoring requires OS-level access (not always available)
- Agent may learn to circumvent pattern matching

**Failure Modes:**
- "Overblocking": too many false positives вҶ’ user disables guardian
- "Pattern blindness": agent phrases dangerous actions to avoid regex matches
- "Normalization drift": anomaly detector adapts to increasingly risky behavior
- "Alert fatigue": too many Level 1 warnings вҶ’ user ignores all levels
- "Circumvention": agent uses eval/exec to bypass tool-level interception
- "Performance drag": monitoring adds 20-200ms per action вҶ’ agent feels sluggish

### Implementation Complexity: **MEDIUM**

- Pattern matcher: regex rules + embedding similarity (off-the-shelf)
- Anomaly detector: statistical models on action embeddings (scikit-learn level)
- Policy engine: simple rules engine (Open Policy Agent or custom)
- Interception hooks: needs agent framework integration (Claude API, OpenAI, etc.)
- ~6-10 weeks for MVP with 2 engineers
- ~100-200 initial patterns needed (crowdsourced + auto-generated from incident data)

---

## Architecture E: Federated Learning Mesh (Cross-Agent Knowledge Sharing Network)

### Core Insight
**Every agent's failures should prevent every other agent's failures. LI becomes a decentralized knowledge mesh where agents continuously share outcome vectors, and the network learns collective intelligence without a central bottleneck.**

This is the most ambitious architecture. LI operates as a distributed protocol вҖ” each agent runs a local LI node that contributes encrypted outcome embeddings to a shared mesh. The mesh aggregates patterns, trains shared models, and distributes learned "instincts" back to every agent. No agent is an island; the network's intelligence compounds with every action taken by any agent, anywhere.

       в”Ӯ                              в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
       в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ REGIONAL HUB          в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ - Aggregate gradients в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ   from cluster nodes  в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ - Federated averaging в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ - Forward to global   в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ                              в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
       в”Ӯ                                          в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ                              в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
       в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ GLOBAL AGGREGATOR      в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ - Aggregate all hubs  в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ - Train global model  в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ - Detect global       в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ   anomaly patterns    в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ - Update reputation   в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ                              в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
       в”Ӯ                                          в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ                              в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
       в”Ӯ  DOWNSTREAM (periodic)        в”Ӯ MODEL DISTRIBUTION    в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ - Push updated weightsв”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ в—„в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ӯ   to all nodes        в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ  Updated global model        в”Ӯ - Push new threat     в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ  New threat signatures       в”Ӯ   signatures          в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ  Reputation updates          в”Ӯ - Push success        в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ   pattern registry    в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ                              в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
       в”Ӯ
       в–ј
  NEXT AGENT ACTION (now informed by global knowledge)
```

### Interception Points

| Point | Mechanism | Scale |
|-------|-----------|-------|
| Local (per action) | Architecture D's Guardian runs locally | 1 agent |
| Batch (per N actions) | Gradient contribution sent to hub | 1 node вҶ’ hub |
| Regional (per cluster) | Federated averaging across 10-100 nodes | Hub level |
| Global (per epoch) | Global model update and distribution | All nodes |
| On-demand | Node queries global knowledge base for novel situations | 1 node вҶ’ global |

### Privacy Architecture

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  PRIVACY GUARANTEES                                          в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                                              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  What leaves the node:                                       в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    вң“ Model gradients (not raw data)                          в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    вң“ Anonymized outcome embeddings (Оө-differential privacy)  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    вң“ Threat signatures (generalized)                         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    вң“ Aggregate statistics (counts, not specifics)            в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                                              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  What stays on the node:                                     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    вң— Raw conversations                                       в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    вң— User data                                               в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    вң— API keys / secrets                                      в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    вң— Specific tool parameters                                в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    вң— PII / sensitive outputs                                 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                                              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Reputation system:                                          в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    - Nodes earn reputation by contributing useful gradients  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    - Low-reputation nodes: gradients downweighted            в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    - Malicious nodes: contributions ignored, flagged         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    - Sybil resistance: node identity tied to agent identity  в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

### Success Metrics

| Metric | Target | Measurement |
|--------|--------|-------------|
| Global model accuracy improvement | +5-15% per 1000 nodes | Held-out evaluation |
| Cold-start mitigation | New node reaches 80% of veteran accuracy in <100 actions | Accuracy vs actions curve |
| Threat signature propagation | Zero-day threat detected in <1 hour across network | Time from first sighting to network-wide block |
| Privacy preservation | <0.1% information leakage (membership inference) | Differential privacy audit |
| Node retention | >90% of nodes active after 30 days | Churn rate |
| Bandwidth efficiency | <10MB/day per node | Data transferred |
| Cross-domain transfer | +20% success on new domain using global model | Novel task type success rate |

### Trade-offs and Failure Modes

**Strengths:**
- Network effects: every new agent makes every other agent smarter
- Privacy-preserving: raw data never leaves the node
- Resilient: no single point of failure (decentralized)
- Scalable: hierarchical aggregation handles millions of nodes
- Cold-start solved: new agents inherit global knowledge immediately
- Novel threat detection: global anomaly detector catches patterns no single node sees
- Works across organizations and agent types

**Weaknesses:**
- Coordination complexity: hierarchical aggregation, consensus, versioning
- Privacy-utility trade-off: stronger privacy = less useful gradients
- Free-rider problem: nodes that only download, never contribute
- Model staleness: global model updates are periodic, not real-time
- Network dependency: offline agents don't benefit from latest updates
- Incentive alignment: why would organizations share outcome data?

**Failure Modes:**
- "Model poisoning": malicious nodes inject bad gradients вҶ’ global model degrades
- "Consensus failure": regional hubs disagree on model update direction
- "Privacy collapse": enough gradient queries reconstruct training data
- "Balkanization": network splits into incompatible model lineages
- "Staleness spiral": update frequency too low вҶ’ global model irrelevant
- "Free-rider collapse": too many consumers, too few contributors вҶ’ network dies
- "Catastrophic forgetting at scale": global model optimized for majority, forgets edge cases

### Implementation Complexity: **VERY HIGH**

- Federated learning framework: modify TensorFlow Federated or PySyft
- Differential privacy: implement DP-SGD with Оө budgeting
- Hierarchical aggregation: custom protocol (gRPC + protobuf)
- Reputation system: blockchain or distributed ledger for trust
- Threat signature distribution: gossip protocol
- ~20-30 weeks for MVP with 4-5 engineers + distributed systems expertise
- Infrastructure: coordination servers, regional hubs, monitoring
- Network bootstrap: need seed nodes and incentive mechanism

---

## Comparative Summary

```
ARCHITECTURE          COMPLEXITY   LATENCY    COST      AGENT TYPES   KEY STRENGTH
в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
A. Hierarchical       HIGH         50-500ms   Med-High  All           Defense in depth;
  Decision Layer      (3 layers)   per step              (templates   catches failures at
                                           needed)       multiple levels

B. Continuous RL      HIGH         100ms-2s   High       All           Theoretically optimal;
  with Sim Rollouts   (ML heavy)   per action  (GPU)     (learns)     learns from simulation

C. Multi-Agent Swarm  MED-HIGH     0-500ms    Med-High   All           Redundancy + parallel
  with Shared Memory   (orchestr.) per task   (N agents)  (profiles)   execution + collective

D. Proactive          MEDIUM       10-100ms   Low-Med    All           Fastest to implement;
  Guardian             (sidecar)    per action (rules)    (universal)   widest threat coverage

E. Federated          VERY HIGH     Async      Low per    All           Network effects;
  Learning Mesh        (distrib.)   (batched)  node        (universal)  scales to millions
```

## Migration Paths

```
CURRENT STATE (MCP Interception)
    в”Ӯ
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ QUICK WIN (2-4 weeks) в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶ Architecture D: Proactive Guardian
    в”Ӯ                                  Add sidecar monitoring + threat rules
    в”Ӯ
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ MEDIUM TERM (6-12 weeks) в”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶ Architecture C: Multi-Agent Swarm
    в”Ӯ                                  Add task decomposition + shared memory
    в”Ӯ
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ AMBITIOUS (12-20 weeks) в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶ Architecture A: Hierarchical Decision Layer
    в”Ӯ                                  Full three-layer defense system
    в”Ӯ
    в””в”Җв”Җ MOONSHOT (20-30 weeks) в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶ Architecture B + E: RL + Federated Mesh
                                       Distributed learning across all agents
```

## Recommendation

**Start with Architecture D (Proactive Guardian) as the immediate upgrade**, then layer Architecture C (Swarm) for multi-agent scenarios, and gradually incorporate elements of Architecture A (Hierarchical) for high-stakes domains. Architecture B (RL) and E (Federated) represent the long-term vision.

**Priority order:**
1. Architecture D вҖ” highest impact-to-effort ratio, protects agents today
2. Architecture C вҖ” enables multi-agent workflows and shared learning
3. Architecture A вҖ” defense in depth for production/critical systems
4. Architecture B вҖ” when you have GPU budget and need optimal action selection
5. Architecture E вҖ” when you reach 1000+ agents and network effects matter

1	# LayerInfinite вҖ” Proactive Guardian Architecture
2	
3	**Version:** 1.0.0
4	**Date:** 2026-05-18
5	**Status:** Design complete, ready for Phase 1 implementation
6	
7	---
8	
9	## 1. Problem Statement
10	
11	LayerInfinite v3.2.0 RECOMMENDS the best action but cannot PREVENT a bad action from executing.
12	
13	The current `li_action` MCP tool returns `approved: false` as plain JSON text (`packages/mcp-server/src/tools/li-action.ts:169-181`). The LLM is free to ignore it. The `auto` mode returns `{action: bestAction}` as a suggestion вҖ” not a hard redirect (`li-action.ts:196-209`). The `policy-engine.ts` has `escalate`, `SANDBOX`, `abstain` вҖ” all advisory labels with no enforcement mechanism.
14	
15	**This architecture upgrades LI from recommendation to enforcement.**
16	
17	---
18	
19	## 2. Guiding Principles
20	
21	1. **Fail-closed, not fail-open.** Uncertain predictions default to BLOCK, not allow.
22	2. **Multi-layer interception.** Four independent interception points ensure coverage regardless of agent framework.
23	3. **Mode-gated strictness.** Same `LAYERINFINITE_MODE` env var controls enforcement strictness вҖ” bootstrap customers see zero friction.
24	4. **Explainable blocks.** Every blocked action returns a machine-readable explanation with signals, weights, and suggested alternatives.
25	5. **Shadow-then-enforce.** Three phases of log-only deployment before any hard block is activated.
26	6. **Zero new infrastructure.** Reuses existing Hono API, Supabase Postgres, LightGBM pipeline, and MCP server.
27	
28	---
29	
30	## 3. Synthesis of External Patterns
31	
32	Three patterns were evaluated as the strongest evidence base for this design:
33	
34	| Pattern | Why It Fits | Evidence |
35	|---------|-------------|----------|
36	| **Banking 3-stage cascade** (Stripe Radar, FICO Falcon) | Rules (<2ms) вҶ’ GBDT (<10ms) вҶ’ async review. LI's stack already maps 1:1. | Stripe Radar: 21% fraud reduction at <1% FPR, <50ms p99 |
37	| **Neural policy distillation** | Train lightweight LightGBM head on `fact_outcomes` to predict P(success \| action, context). Already have the data and model runtime. | DeepMind AlphaGo policy net; ad ranking вҖ” 1-2% AUC loss vs deep model, 100-1000x faster |
38	| **Constrained contextual bandits** | Wilson lower-bound column in recommendations + delta-risk threshold. Probabilistic safety without changing deterministic core. | LinkedIn feed, Netflix artwork вҖ” 5-20% lift with probabilistic safety guarantees |
39	
40	Patterns rejected: Constitutional AI (wrong harm model вҖ” LI blocks operational failure, not toxicity), formal verification (not real-time), NeMo Guardrails as-is (adds LLM latency).
41	
42	---
43	
44	## 4. Architecture Overview
45	
46	### 4.1 Four Interception Points
47	
48	```
49	                          Agent (LLM + tools)
50	                               |
51	        +----------------------+----------------------+
52	        | INTERCEPTION LAYER   |   (any can trigger) |
53	        |                      v                      |
54	        |  [IP1] Prompt-guard    [IP2] MCP li-guardian |
55	        |  [IP3] API gateway     [IP4] SDK interceptor |
56	        +----------------------+----------------------+
57	                               | GuardianRequest
58	                               v
59	                  +---------------------------+
60	                  |      GUARDIAN CORE        |
61	                  |                           |
62	                  |  1. FastRulePath   <10ms  |
63	                  |       v no-hit            |
64	                  |  2. MLGuard       <100ms  |
65	                  |       v uncertain         |
66	                  |  3. PolicyEngine  <1ms    |
67	                  |       v                   |
68	                  |  4. ConfidenceGate        |
69	                  |     fail-closed -> BLOCK  |
70	                  +-----------+---------------+
71	                              | Decision
72	              +-------+-------+-------+-------+
73	              v       v       v       v       v
74	            ALLOW   WARN   REWRITE  BLOCK  ESCALATE
75	              |       |       |       |       |
76	              +-------+-------+-------+-------+
77	                              v
78	                   audit_trail + outcome_log
79	                              |
80	              +---------------+---------------+
81	              v                               v
82	       ASYNC MCTS enrichment        Counterfactual training
83	       (>5s, non-blocking)          (blocks become negative rows)
84	```
85	
86	| IP | When It Fires | Location | Enforcement Strength |
87	|----|--------------|----------|---------------------|
88	| **IP1: Prompt-guard** | During LLM reasoning, before tool selection | MCP tool `li-guardian` + customer-side prompt middleware | Advisory вҖ” LLM may ignore |
89	| **IP2: MCP `li-guardian`** | At `li_action` tool call | `packages/mcp-server/src/tools/li-guardian.ts` | Advisory вҖ” returns block signal as text |
90	| **IP3: API gateway** | Outbound HTTP from agent framework | `layer5/api/middleware/guardian-gateway.ts` | Hard вҖ” intercepts before request leaves agent infra |
91	| **IP4: SDK interceptor** | Within agent's runtime | `layer5/sdks/typescript/src/interceptor.ts` | **Hard** вҖ” monkey-patches tool dispatcher, throws before execution |
92	
93	**Critical insight:** Only IP4 can truly hard-block. The others return advisory signals that the agent framework must honor. LI ships a thin interceptor that the customer wraps around their tool executor. If guardian says `blocked: true`, the interceptor throws before the action reaches the backend.
94	
95	### 4.2 Decision Pipeline (<200ms p99)
96	
97	| Stage | What It Does | Latency | Short-Circuit |
98	|-------|-------------|---------|---------------|
99	| **Fast rules** | Aho-corasick + regex on action name, params, headers. Patterns: shell injection, secret leakage, destructive verbs, prod-resource match. Per-customer compiled ruleset. | <10ms | BLOCK immediately on match |
100	| **ML guard** | Logistic regression predicting failure probability from 24 features (trust score, drift, context match, recency, semantic cluster). Weights loaded from `ml_guard_models` table. | <100ms (cached) | BLOCK if p(fail) > 0.85 |
101	| **Policy engine** | Existing decision tree extended with guardian signals. Remains pure-function. | <1ms | ESCALATE if uncertain |
102	| **Confidence gate** | Fail-closed: if \|p - 0.5\| < margin AND mode in {assist, auto}, BLOCK by default | <1ms | Never allows uncertain predictions |
103	
104	### 4.3 Fail-Closed Design
105	
106	If the ML model is uncertain (`|p - 0.5| < 0.2`), the default is **BLOCK** вҖ” not allow.
107	
108	When blocking, the response includes:
109	```json
110	{
111	  "blocked": true,
112	  "reason": "predicted_failure_probability=0.94",
113	  "signals": [
114	    {"source": "regex", "pattern": "#credential-leak", "weight": 0.4},
115	    {"source": "model", "feature": "trust_score", "value": 0.12, "weight": 0.35},
116	    {"source": "drift", "feature": "context_mismatch", "value": true, "weight": 0.25}
117	  ],
118	  "suggested_action": "escalate_to_human"
119	}
120	```
121	
122	### 4.4 Mode-Aware Strictness
123	
124	Same `LAYERINFINITE_MODE` env var controls guardian strictness:
125	
126	| Mode | Guardian Behavior |
127	|------|-------------------|
128	| `bootstrap` | Log only вҖ” guardian evaluates but never blocks. Builds training data silently. |
129	| `recommend` | Warn + return `suggested_action` вҖ” no enforcement |
130	| `assist` | Suggest + escalate on high-risk вҖ” soft enforcement (returns `human_review_required: true`) |
131	| `auto` | **BLOCK if predicted failure > threshold** вҖ” hard enforcement via SDK interceptor |
132	
133	---
134	
135	## 5. File-Level Implementation Plan

139	**Guardian core module: `layer5/api/lib/guardian/`**

141	| File | Responsibility | Lines (est) |

182	
183	## 6. Phased Migration (10 Weeks)
184	
185	### Phase 0 вҖ” Security Blockers (Before Any Enforcement)
186	
187	Before Phase 4 turns on hard blocking, fix these critical security issues from the v3.2.0 audit:
188

193	### Phase 1 вҖ” Fast-Path Rules (Weeks 1-2)
194
